summary: Straight from the Duotone Laboratory. Aluula D/TX and Trinity TX construction, pulley-free bridle for crisper bar feel, shortened chord for vertical takeoff, and refined arc for faster loop recovery. The ultimate Rebel DNA distilled into Aluula.
best_for: Serious big air riders wanting the ultimate Rebel

sources.summary: The Aluula D/TX makes this a different animal. More vertical boost, faster recovery, and the pulley-free bridle gives a precision feel no other kite in this category matches.
If you're serious about big air and have the budget, this is the top of the mountain for the Rebel line.

reviews.video_title: Big Air Kite Review 23 - 2026 Duotone Rebel D/Lab
reviews.excerpt: Hello and welcome to another plus-size kite review where in this episode I'll be covering the latest Duo Tone Rebel Dab with the help of some of my local friends and feedback from other Rebel riders. I'll discuss our experiences riding it back to back with the previous model. Also [snorts] taking a
reviews.verdict: sure to like and subscribe and drop a comment on what you thought of the review, what other kites you are waiting to see. Um um I do have a few that I'm already working on and trying to get hold of others. So your comments really do help um me make you know brands and shops want to send me gear. So uh thank you so much for always supporting and following along. If you want to see what I'm riding at the moment, check out my Instagram as always, Jason of Montreal. And until the next time, goodbye.
reviews.full_transcript: Hello and welcome to another plus-size kite review where in this episode I'll be covering the latest Duo Tone Rebel Dab with the help of some of my local friends and feedback from other Rebel riders. I'll discuss our experiences riding it back to back with the previous model. Also [snorts] taking a look a little bit about how it compares to the recent Evo Dab. and as always try to compare them all with the rest of the big air market. I will also be going into more depth on the two EVOs with a backto-back comparison between the two of them and the more recent EVO SLS in my next review also coming soon once we get a little bit more wind. We are currently in summer right now, which is why 80% of my sessions have been on [music] the Fly Surfer Sonic Fives recently, and it's been a little bit of a struggle to get on the nines for some proper big air. Now, these reviews once again are only made possible with the help of the great team at Mac Kiteboarding. If you're new to this channel or just enjoy my [music] reviews, then please be sure to check out their YouTube channel for a ridiculous amount of awesome kite content. And if you're looking for new gear, be sure to check out their website, which I have put some links in the description. It's now been 2 years with the team at Mac supporting these reviews with uh demo gear, and always they've [music] just been the coolest guys to work with. So, a huge thanks to them as always. You'll also notice that measuring my jump height and general progression. As usual, I'm using the Surfer app um because I do find it the most reliable way to track your height [music] and trick progression. because of the fixed position on your body via cell phone tucked into your wets suit or the wrist position uh via your watch. Uh [music] for my friends and I, it does the best job to consistently measure our different styles of jumps no [music] matter if we are looping, doing board offs, rotations, or whatever tricks. Of course, we found that uh more and more using the surfer app motivates us to focus more on our progression because we know whatever type of trick we go for [music] and put our body position in, we'll get the same read uh with the height as if we just do a straight boost. [music] So, the post session chat is just really about how high our biggest jump were, but we also love to break down how high our biggest loop was or how high our biggest board off or rotation, etc., [music] etc. It's just a really nice added uh element to kiting. I'll also just add a quick mention that if you like this content and want to help support it, there is a donations page in the description where you can send me a tip. All of the money from those tips goes right back into getting hold of [music] new gear uh and any travel costs to chase the wind around New Zealand to make more reviews. So, with all of that housekeeping out of the way, let's get on to talking about these bloody amazing kites. So, first off, if you haven't already seen my previous review on the original Rebel DAB, then you should check that out on my channel. >> [music] >> And since that review, I've had the benefit of riding both of last year Rebels more with many of the other top kites on the market, as well as having more feedback from local friends who now ride them and are themselves hitting new PBS and progressing with the kites. In the repeat sessions on the older Model Dab, I've found that I've actually liked it more and more as I've gotten more [music] used to its bar pressure, its power, and general behavior. Although all the points I made in the original review are still accurate, I've started to find I do like this general style a bit more, at least for big air on smaller sizes, say 8 or 9 m, as the kite is still fast enough to be good for kite loops at my level, but not so fast that when [music] I do go for board offs or rotations with or without kite loops, I'm not as often moving the kite too fast or too far out of position as I can find myself with faster, lighter kites. As most riders will know, it's often easier to progress with non-kite loop related tricks on larger [music] kites first because they are slower, have more hang time, and are harder to move out of position while jumping or traveling through the air. Here in New Zealand, we get perhaps 5 to 10 times more [music] 10 and 12 m days than we do 8 or 9 m days. So, I'm always just more used to flying larger sized kites, which means [music] taking the skills that I learn on the larger sizes down to smaller kites in stronger winds can be very challenging, particularly when we're not on them that often and when the kites are faster and more loop orientated. Were I to live in somewhere like northeast Brazil or Cape Town where I were a much lighter person and could ride eight or nine meter kites for most of my sessions, then it would probably be a different story and I'd be more likely to be able to get a higher percentage of my progression in on smaller kites. But as it stands, uh it's the bigger kites that always seem to be where I land things for the [music] first and then I work them down outside of looping, of course. A great example of a rider I know who chases stronger winds year round to ride on smaller kites, but has still been finding the easier to handle balance of speed and power of the Rebel Dab [music] is a good buddy of mine, Dark Slide Dave, who you may recall from the Core Pace Pro review. Dave has been on the Rebel DLABS now for a year, having previously been on the Pace Pro and Orbits [music] prior to that. Now, his level was already high before he got onto the Rebel with all variety of loops and of course slides long enough to end up in the car park amongst his bag of tricks. But since moving on to the Rebels, he's found that not only does it make him impactfully grunt like a professional woman's tennis player, but he's also been getting more consistent [music] height, and the kites have helped him get more consistent with most of his tricks that he already had while also over time once getting used to the added pressure of the power of [music] the rebels. They've allowed him to add numerous technical elements to those tricks like [music] new variations of boardoffs and rotations with them coming in his loops and [music] things like that. Of course, he could have learned these on his previous kites, [music] but like myself, he's found the added hang time and fairly predictable, stable, and not too crazy fast looping style of the Red Bull a real benefit to helping him progress. And as a bonus, it's also helped him crush his surfer PB multiple times as well since making the switch. Now, as I mentioned in the previous videos, if you are not already a very proficient [music] and confident looper, the power and energy it will take you to get into loops [music] on the Rebels is always going to be a turnoff. But there have been some changes to this new model [music] that are certainly making it more accessible. and even your greener looping progressor uh is going to find it beneficial in this regard. So, of course, if you are a rider who is already used to riding smaller sizes of faster kites that are lighter on the bar, [music] then it's not like you're going to magically jump on a Rebel and find it the bees knees right away. The good news for those riders who do like a lighter, more playful, direct, and faster style kite, but are still curious what the joy of that Rebel power can bring into their lives. Well, this new version has done a fairly solid job of getting the Rebel a little bit closer to that more dynamic direct style without too greatly compromising the balls to the wall Rebel feel. With each session I had, I found the wind was starting quite low in the early 20s, so I could get a little feel for the light to mid-strength wind and it would consistently increase up over the 35 knot mark. So, I got some full power on the 9 uh in each of the sessions [music] and plenty of loops in the 10 to 15 m range with even some 20 m sends on my surfer. Going [music] from the older model to the newer one, the steering pressure was noticeably lighter and more direct on the newer model, which [music] made the kite generally easier to pilot. The removal of the pulleys and the new wing tip attachments gave you [music] a more direct feel with this kite and allowed you to feel a little bit more of it and also added some responsiveness when engaging the bar. In fact, when you were out in the lighter to medium strength conditions, say 20 to 30 knots, at least for a rider my size on the 9 m, you have to wonder why they were ever on there at [music] all. But that is at least until the wind starts to crank up over 30 or more knots [music] and you're made all too aware that without the give from the pulleys, the more direct feel and connection to the kite also means you get to feel more of each gust. And the top end can end up being a little bit harder to handle, at least as you ride back and forth. And particularly when you start to load up a hard edge to get line tension to boost. It also means that when you're looking for a bit of a breather just by letting out the bar while you are heavily powered or even sheeting out the deep power, you're not going to be given quite as much uh of relief um or release from the pull as you will with the previous model. So although the steering pressure is lighter, you don't seem to have quite the same ability to feel as powered down in the very strong stuff as the older model. Where the newer model does improve on the previous at the top end is that because the kite is more direct, experienced riders should find it easier to time the gusts when going to boost. And as you're airborne, aside from the direct connection you have with the kite, which makes it easier to time your kite's movements through the sky, the new material also feels like it has a little bit more flex to it. and [music] with an even cleaner, smoother looping feel, a bit more like an improved version of the SLS I reviewed in 2025, which when compared to the older DAB, I did find a bit easier in the loops. So, with some experiments with the older model where we took off the pulleys to see how it felt, [music] um although decent and lighter winds, when things got up there, the kites just seem to work better for us with the pulleys on on the older model. My assumption with why this is is that the older gold Alula is just a bit more rigid and thus doesn't work as well [music] as the new material to shield you from some of the power and the [music] flexibility of the new material when the wind is stronger. So, [music] how does this performance stack up in the key areas such as boosting, looping, and confidence building? Well, starting as always with boosting, there is no doubt this kite is going to be one of the best boosting kites ever made. But you already knew it would be because of the price tag and the fact it's a Rebel. Now, by comparison to the previous, I'd say the boosting potential of these two kites was so similar that it is impossible to say for sure which of the two would make most riders go bigger. But of my friends, they felt like it was the older model, which they of course have a lot more time on. But I wasn't as convinced it did for me. Although I would agree that I tend to think for most riders it might be the older model with the slight edge. I think the boosting sweet spot on that older model is just a little bit easier to find. Uh which could of course be due to it not shooting as quickly when you engage it to send it. um which they are more used to as well whereas I tend to be riding a lot of different 9 m kites that are particularly quicker uh often the Blainechild kites for example and find I often boost the best with kites that I have a bit more connection and that have a more direct feel for some reason. I found this most obvious when I rode the Peak [music] versus the Trigger for example. Although there is no question the peak is the better overall booster than [music] the trigger, for whatever reason, with my style and timing, in each of the sessions I rode those kites back to back, I just would always go slightly higher on surfer each time with the trigger. When it comes, of course, to hang time again, the older model just felt like it had the slight edge. But once again, how much of that is from this kite responding to us a little slower and thus our helies were a little wider uh because it wasn't being pulled so quickly. Um but the difference even in hang time was also marginal if at all which keeps this newer model as one of the best boosting and hangtime kites out there even if you need to be a little bit more precise to access [music] both of those areas. So, with boosting and hangtime being amazing, as you'd expect, let's move on to where the latest upgrades shine more clearly for me, which is [music] the looping. Now, of course, the kite is still a powerheavy rebel. So, the loops are always going to be more intimidating at first [music] than many of your entry-level loopers, but when comparing like for like, it is certainly, in my opinion, the best and easiest looping rebel that I have ever tried. This does not make it on par, of course, with entrylevel loopers like the Nitro, Orbits, or ERA or the faster can loop almost any size kites like the Brainchild models, the be the Thrive, Trigger, [music] um, or the Reeden kites, uh, or even the, uh, lighter, faster three strut Alula kites like the Vortex or Pace. But for the power and the hang time that you [music] get out of this kite, it is a vast improvement over its predecessors. The nearest comparison in terms of looping style would of course be the Harlem Peak, which uh to be fair, we did ride many months apart, but the [music] Peak does have a very similar overall style to the Rebel with a lot of power [music] behind it. I would still say the peak feels a little friendlier on the bar and it would be faster through the loops. Uh, thus making going for loops more confidence boosting with the peak. But I don't recall the peak being quite as direct as this Rebel. So for uh myself and my friends, although the new Rebel is still probably not quite as fast, it's more direct nature made it easier to time the loops once you got through the loop and were going for the heli [music] loops, particularly as you were coming down, which uh made the kite just a little bit easier to track as it went overhead and thus making a little bit less uh technical and made catching it a a little bit more consistent for me. Both of these kites though, for the amount of power and hang [music] time you get are excellent uh loopers. So, with all of the improvements I've mentioned, from the general feel to its looping ability, as you'd expect, that also translates very nicely to the confidence [music] boosting nature of the kite for big air when compared to its predecessors. [music] uh at least in the lighter to moderately strong winds of course [music] um as most of us would expect to be making the most of their technical uh and skills progress in those terms. Certainly the more skilled rider would benefit from it uh in the higher end wins as well, especially with a bit more time on it versus the previous one. Of course, as with most big power monsters, there is still a fair bit of bar pressure that will require some nerve to handle going for new tricks with. [music] But despite still being uh in the seat harness due to injury, uh I was constantly fighting the urge to go for some rotations and to see how I fared with this Rebel and was certainly less tentative than I ever was with the original model. As you can see here in this uh old DAB clip that took a little bit of courage for me to muster up to go for. I found the overall improvements also helped my confidence to throw some larger loops in each direction. Um even though I am fairly competent looping in both ways. But that was uh very nice I found um on this particular Rebel being in the seat harness and obviously tentative uh coming back from injury. [music] Now, I wouldn't move it up to the levels of confidence builders like the NXT or the Vortex um or a number of the usual Dacron kites that are probably better suited for progressing loopers just getting into their first loops. um that offer her noticeably less power, but it's certainly going to be a kite that most riders will find they can push their level as well as progress their loops and of course push their height measurements, which is where obviously the kite shines the greatest, [music] which is a fantastic development for this model. When stacking it up against the other top kites I've rode in the last year, it is absolutely in the conversation for the best bigger kite of the year. certainly at least in the smaller sizes. Um, if you know me by now, uh, you'll know I generally tend to prefer three strut kites in sizes 10 and up with some of the brainchild models being the exception there, sneaking into that region for their lightweight construction. But I could still see this uh version of the Rebel being an excellent option for a 10 m, perhaps even 11 or 12. Um, but having uh been also recently riding the latest Evo Dab, I'd have to say if I was going for an all duo tone quiver, then um I'd definitely want to be going with the Rebel in sizes 7 through 9 for myself. um then I'd probably start swapping to the Evos from around 10 or 11 m and up for my personal riding style and preferences. As we do get a lot more light wind days from sort of 15 to 25 knots than we do where it will blow stronger than that. The [music] benefits of the low-end side of the EVO certainly have the edge for me over the Rebel in the uh bigger sizes. Now, I will be doing a more detailed review on both the the latest Evo DLAB and EVO SLS next, comparing it to the previous model and how it sort of fits in. After that, I also have a review comparing my 12 m Evo Dab to the 12 m Sonic. Um because I have been using the Sonics more than any other kite this summer due to the amount of light wind we have and I've been really enjoying the 12 meter a lot more than I did the 15, which I still enjoy when I have the opportunity to ride it. So, lots to come. Um as always, thank you so much for making it this far. Please be sure to like and subscribe and drop a comment on what you thought of the review, what other kites you are waiting to see. Um um I do have a few that I'm already working on and trying to get hold of others. So your comments really do help um me make you know brands and shops want to send me gear. So uh thank you so much for always supporting and following along. If you want to see what I'm riding at the moment, check out my Instagram as always, Jason of Montreal. And until the next time, goodbye.

reviews.full_transcript: Welcome back guys to this brand new Kaimana video. And today we have something very special. The brand new 2026 Duotone Rebel Dab for them life. When I step into the jungle they want to group up. Never move up. Never going to win a rumble. But when I come through, you know I love I send shots for your team and leader like Joe. >> We arrived at the Zamp motor. We brought a 9 m 2026 Rebel Dlab and we're going to compare it together with Jamie Overbake. So now wait for the boys, gear up and head to the spot. Let's go. Hi guys, it's Jamie here. Wind is looking good. I think we have around 30 knots. It's going to be good on 9 m. The lagoon is a bit small, but it will probably get a little bit bigger in the next hour. So, yeah, let's try to send it and see how the new Rebel Dab performs. I hope you're as excited as we are cuz this kite seems to be a crazy good kite. So, let's talk about all the technical details first and then head out to the water and see what this guide is all about. >> Okay, before we head back to the zone motor for some more action, I'm here to tell you a little bit more information about the Rebel Dab. So, the Rebel DAB for 2025 was the first Rebel DAB in Alula material and it was super successful. It was even a thing that inside the company there was a certain target that they wanted to hit in number of sales and in October and November this number was reached already. So, everybody was blown away by the results. Everybody in the industry really likes the kite. Everybody that jumps it really likes the kite. You see Andrea jumping super big. All the team riders. I jumped over a plane with the kite, which was super cool. For 2026, there were a few kind of key features that they wanted to change. One of them is to change to the Duotone DTX Alula material, which is a new material exclusive to Duotone. They wanted to make the kite a little bit more playful, so it doesn't fly straight as much, but it steers faster. They wanted more vertical jumping, so you can jump straight up instead of floating downwind, but still trying to maintain the the hang time in the kite. and they wanted to ideally remove the pulleys of the bridal. All those things were a very big challenge especially for the designer and a lot of work. Removing the pulley is one of the hardest things you can do in a kite design especially once you have a good kite with a pulley but then taking the pulley off you just basically start all over again. But all the things have been hurt. So having the kite with the Alula DTX material which is a little bit more flexible and removing the pulleys kind of made it the same as how it was before with the stiffer material and the pulleys. So that that's kind of how they made it more playful uh but still very stable. And another thing they did is they shortened the center strut slightly which kind of makes the aspect a little bit bigger. And then with the more flexible material it makes the kite much faster. So you can see in the videos where maybe jumping with the old kite you could do like one big down loop. And now with the newer Rebel Dab it's much more faster. It's very intuitive and you can very well kind of decide if you want to do a big loop or a small loop. The the bar feedback is super nice. So you can just crank hard and do a little loop or do it a little bit softer and do a bigger loop and it will keep going very fast and keep a lot of forward momentum. So in theory from the testing and everything we've done, the handling should be much better. The steering should be quicker. The turning rate is could be a little bit quicker, but the real action and the real knowledge can be when Lucas and Jamie try it out on the Z motor and see what they say cuz they don't know what I'm saying right now. They actually had the session already and now I'm saying this. So we'll see. Let's switch over there. >> So the kites are rigged up. We have the 2025 and the 26 laying here. Jamie is going to go on his trusty old 2025. And afterwards, we're going to switch and see what the differences are. So, let's dive in. So, first impression, the kites are quite a lot different, more than I expected to be honest. And Jamie is also having quite a good time on the 26 version. >> So, Jamie, first impression. It feels super nice. Has a lot of second lift. The bar pressure is a lot lighter than on the old ones. The only thing I'm trying to figure out is how high it jumps. >> Let's see. Let's see. >> The main differences that you can immediately feel is the bar pressure and the steering pressure. The 2025 one has quite a bit more bar pressure cuz you still want to control and uh yeah, a good feeling in the bar if you're going extreme and as high as possible. Uh just like Jamie does over here. Groovy is built. I think that was like 20 m. >> So, we switched again. I'm on the uh 26 one now. So, let's see. Yeah, the old one is also working right now. Yeah, maybe I have to switch back one more time to the new one to see if the wind got different again. But, uh, the low end is better for sure on the new one. >> So, Jamie changed the setting on to the hard setting. He thinks that he can like get the kite more vertical up in the sky. So, he's going to try it right now. So, let's see. This guy never ces as well. It's crazy. I think he still has dry air from the session. Oh, there he comes. Wa. >> Too easy. >> Too easy for him. So, we're back. Let's dive in. We tested a 2025 Rebel Dlab against the 2026 one. Very cool to do that with Jamie. Jeremy was very hyped about the kite as well. So, he will tell you everything about it right now. and afterwards we're going to switch back to me and I'm going to tell you everything about uh how I experienced the two kites. So, let's >> So, I just had my first session on the new Rebel DAB 9 m with 24 m lines. Uh and I was kind of impressed in the beginning. I it felt like everything was better. Uh but after that, I found a few things that that was better on the old one. Uh but overall, the new one is a lot easier. I had more confidence to do my loop tricks. Especially a signature trick for me, Contra U tornado that I really like. For me, it's hard to to do that trick on the old Rebel DAP. And on the new one, it was quite easy because the bar pressure is a bit lighter. Um, and the steering is just more predictable. You can really feel the kite where it's steering. It's super direct. Uh, you really know what the kite is going to do. So, for progression, that one is a lot better. Um, and maybe when the wind really really picks up, the old one can jump a bit more vertical, but overall, if you want to just progress your tricks and stuff, uh, and have a fun session and don't get a tennis elbow, then the new one is a lot better than the old one. So, I really enjoyed it and I'm going to test it a lot more in the future to see in what conditions I really like it. So, uh, we arrived at the spot. The wind was 25 to 30 knots. So, ideal conditions to test these 9 m rebels, especially on flat water. Probably the best conditions to test these kites back to back. Well, you can switch them very quickly on the beach and go out again. Jamie started on the 2025 one. I started on the 2026 one. We did that because Jamie already is familiar with the 2025 Rebel. I rode it as well, but yeah, he could really get the feeling for the 2026 right after we switched. Now, I already like the 2025 Rebel Dlab. I rode a couple times with it and I had great sessions on the kite. So, I was very curious to find out how the 2026 one would perform if there was going to be a difference. Anyway, now we already knew that the Alula was different. But, yeah, maybe the difference were not that big. But, there is a really big difference, especially yeah, in the riding and the bar feel, which I'm going to tell you about later. If you already like the 2025 Rebel DAB, the difference is in a very positive way to the 261. So, the first thing you will notice when you ride the kites back to back is that the 2026 one has lighter bar pressure and steering pressure. It means that you yeah, you can loop the kite easier and it feels easier in your hands compared to the 2025 one. There are still some pressure and you can tune it to the medium setting. That's what Jamie did as well during the session which is quite interesting. So, you can play around with that. All in all, I think that's a perfect mix between light bar pressure but not too light. You don't know where the kite is in the sky. So you still know uh if you're backwards uh yeah in the sky, you still know where the kite is and what it's going to do. So it's not so light that you don't know where the kite is. And otherwise you can even trim it to more the 2025 kind of feel. So another big change in the Rebel Dab 2026 is the turning speed of the kite. So if you pull the bar, the kite really accelerates a bit more than the 2025 one and recovers very fast after kite loops. Yeah, this also makes sure like Lassa said uh in the tech talk is that you can control how you loop the kite. So you can go for a yeah classic rebel dlab loop I would say with a bit more freef fall in it. So you could also go for a new school loop which not a big of an angle while looping it but it recovers quicker after you and you do have some second lift after that. So if you want to do some tricks after after the loop or during the loop yeah this guide really helps you with that. So, also quite funny uh is that I tried a laidback on the 2026 one, which I did not do on the 25. On the 25, I needed to get some riding back and forth to get the feeling in it and the courage to do a kite loop layback. On the 26, I knew that I was going to be coming around with and yeah, get catched with a down loop. You will also get it with a 25, but the comfort on the 26 is yeah, just a bit more cuz you definitely know that you will be coming around after the loop. Yeah. Yeah. And also you you feel it without even looking at it that it's going to go well without a big crash. So all in all in terms of big air, these two kites are uh yeah very very good and outstanding in its range. The spot was not too big and Jamie and I really noticed that the 2026 one has a very good second lift. So the spot nearly was too small to to land on still. Yeah, it was really nice to have like the second lift and go again up in the sky. And the wind even wasn't that strong. Yeah, was a nice benefit of the 261. So, in terms of the D power and how you can control the kite when the wind is going to be a little bit stronger, we did not notice any major differences or actually none. Um, that's a good thing in my uh my opinion because the 2025 one has a pulley bridal and the 26 went to a pulleyless bridal which helps with the directness of the kite. Yeah. How snappy it feels. And sometimes the uh downside of a pulleyless bridle is that it doesn't have as much deep power stroke as the pulley bridle would have. So you maybe could have some differences uh in high-end performance. So when the wind picked up a bit from a raincloud, yeah, we did not notice any differences and we could just ride the same as we did on the 25 or the 26 one. So, in my opinion, that's a very big plus point of the Rebel DLAP 2026 because you have the directness of a a pulleyless bridle and still the range of a pulley bridle as we found on the 2025 one.
